text: Open nephro-ureterectomy remains a standard for treatment of upper tract transitional cell carcinoma. Laparoscopic nephro-ureterectomy was performed in an effort to decrease patient morbidity However, adhering to the oncologic principle of removal of a bladder cuff was found to be very difficult using the laparoscopic approach. Robotic nephro-uterectomy provides the morbidity advantage of a minimally invasive approach, but improves upon the laparoscopic technique by allowing complete removal of the bladder cuff and sutured closure of the bladder. It also provides complete exposure of the entire retroperitoneum. The current robotic platform, the DaVinci XI, provides complete access to the abdomen and pelvis using four robotic arms and a single docking approach. The improved access also facilitates retroperitoneal lymph node dissection of the perihilar lymph nodes.

To demonstrate our technique of robotic nephro-ureterectomy, we present the case of a seventy-two-year-old female who presented with gross hematuria and on CT scan was found to have a one point seven centimeter nodule in the lower pole calyx. Ureteroscopic biopsy revealed high-grade transitional cell carcinoma and metastatic evaluation with chest imaging was negative. The patient had normal renal function. Given these findings, our plan was for left robotic nephro-ureterectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection using the da Vinci XI. The procedure is performed with the patient in a modified flank position, and a linear four-port configuration is used with the ports angled medially to allow complete access to the pelvis. The robot is docked midway between the kidney and bladder over the iliac crest.

The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the colon and dissecting the mesocolon off Drota's fascia. The ureter is identified, and the mid-ureter is clipped but not divided to prevent tumor cell migration into the bladder. The fourth robotic arm provides traction on the ureter, enhancing exposure in the pelvis.

The surgeon dissects the ureter into the pelvis until encountering the detrusor fibers, indicated by the widening of the ureter as it enters the bladder. The bladder mucosa is entered, and a barbed suture is anchored to the bladder opening. The bladder cuff is sequentially released with scissors and sutured to prevent retraction. The ureteral orifice is identified, and the bladder cuff is completely released. The cystotomy is closed in two layers using the barbed suture, and the bladder is filled to ensure a watertight closure.

The surgeon focuses on the kidney, isolating the renal vein and artery. The renal artery and vein are divided using an endoscopic stapler. The dissection plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal gland is developed, preserving the adrenal gland in situ. The posterior and lateral attachments of the kidney are divided, allowing the kidney to be released.

The surgeon performs retroperitoneal lymph node dissection by isolating the periaortic lymphatics, starting at the renal hilum and moving inferiorly. Polymer clips are used to control the lymphatic tissue surrounding the aorta, and the inferior extent of the lymphatic packet is secured with additional clips. The lymphatic packet is then placed into an endoscopic retrieval bag.

Operative time was two point five hours and the patient was discharged on post-operative day three Final pathology revealed high-grade transitional cell carcinoma with invasion into the renal parenchyma with negative margins and negative lymph nodes. Pathologic stage was T-three and zero. Postoperatively, the patient's EGFR fell to the expected level, and there was no complication. At follow-up of twenty-four months, there was no evidence of recurrence. Our experience at Swedish Medical Center with nephro-ureterectomy includes thirty-five robotic procedures, with the majority being performed using the da Vinci XI. Mean operative time for the group was just over four hours, with minimal blood loss. The mean length of stay was two point seven days, and the average number of lymph nodes removed was eleven. In conclusion, robotic nephrourectomy offers the advantage of reduced morbidity compared to the open approach, but with superior reach and reconstructive capabilities compared to pure laparoscopy. This allows the complete removal of the bladder cuff and performance of the retroperitoneal lymph node dissection in keeping with the oncologic principles established by open nephrourectomy.
